children.name: Mesitornis
children.description: Medium-sized, terrestrial birds endemic to Madagascar with a slender, slightly curved bill and a long, graduated tail. They are elusive, secretive inhabitants of the forest floor, more often heard than seen.

children.name: Mesitornis variegatus
children.description: A handsome ground bird with a white breast, chestnut flanks and a bold white supercilium, found only in the lowland rainforests of eastern Madagascar. It moves silently through the leaf litter in pairs or small family groups, gleaning insects and small invertebrates. Deforestation has made it one of Madagascar's most threatened endemic birds.

children.name: Mesitornis unicolor
children.description: Endemic to the humid forests of eastern Madagascar, this uniformly brown mesite lacks the bold markings of its cousin and relies on near-invisibility on the dark forest floor. It is extremely shy and difficult to observe, creeping away silently through the undergrowth when disturbed. Its whistled contact calls help family groups stay together in the dense vegetation.

children.name: Monias
children.description: A monotypic genus restricted to the arid spiny forests of southern Madagascar, showing pronounced sexual dimorphism unlike the other mesite genus. It has a longer, more decurved bill adapted for probing in dry soil.

children.name: Monias benschi
children.description: Inhabits the harsh, spiny thickets of southern Madagascar, where it forages on the ground in small groups for seeds, fruits and invertebrates. Unlike the forest mesites, males have a distinctive pale blue patch around the eye and a black breast band, while females are more uniformly sandy-brown. It is vulnerable to habitat destruction from charcoal production and agriculture.
